title: `podman machine start` stuck "Waiting for VM" indefinitely with qemu synchronous exception MacOS M3
body:
### Issue Description

With a fresh install via homebrew, I cannot start a machine and it hangs indefinitely at `Waiting for VM`. Running with debug shows that qemu hits a `Synchronous Exeception at 0x00000000BC308000`. 

### Steps to reproduce the issue

Steps to reproduce the issue
1. brew install podman
2. podman machine init
3. podman machine start


### Describe the results you received

VM fails to start.

### Describe the results you expected

VM starts.

### podman info output

```yaml
Version: 4.7.2
OS: macOS 14.2
Chip: Apple M3 Pro
Architecture: arm64
```


### Podman in a container

No

### Privileged Or Rootless

None

### Upstream Latest Release

Yes

comments.body: I had this issue; updating qemu from 8.1.3_1 to 8.1.3_2 via `brew upgrade qemu` fixed it.

Updating to qemu 8.1.3_2 resulted in a different exception and hang:
```
ASSERT [ArmCpuDxe] /home/kraxel/projects/qemu/doms/edk2/ArmPkg/Library/DefaultExceptionHandlerLib/AArch64/DefaultExceptionHandler.c(333): ((BOOLEAN)(0==1))
```

Reading through some other issues I've found this workaround: `podman machine init -m 3072`

podman 4.7.2
qemu 8.1.3_2
MacOs 14.1.1 - M3 Max

I have intermittently been able to get it to work between sessions of removing everything, re-installing, deleting and recreating machines. Once it decides to work, it is fine until the mac goes to sleep while the QEMU machine is running.

On recovery from sleep, the podman machine shows as running, but it's not accessible. From then on the issues remains until some series of uninstalling and reinstalling, deleting and recreating machines finally works. I have not yet figured it out.

The specific problem seems to be with `-netdev socket,id=vlan,fd=3` if replacing `fd=3` with `listen=:1234` the machine starts, but of course does not work with podman.

EDIT: Steps that seem to make it work again:

1. `podman machine rm`
2. `brew uninstall podman`
3. `brew uninstall qemu`
4. Reboot
5. `brew install podman` (qemu installs with it)
6. Create machine from podman desktop but not let it start it
7. `podman machine start`
I have tried all the methods, but all do not work.

Is this not a result of this issue tracked on the qemu project, which is the result of a bug even further upstream?

https://gitlab.com/qemu-project/qemu/-/issues/1990

I agree with @herblet. Also see the fix employed by Lima: https://github.com/lima-vm/lima/pull/2061

What worked for me:
- Download https://github.com/lima-vm/edk2-patched.tmp/releases/download/edk2-stable202311%2Blima.0/edk2-aarch64-code.fd.gz from https://github.com/lima-vm/edk2-patched.tmp/releases
- Init podman machine
- Update podman machine config json using
  `sed -i 's@file=.*edk2-aarch64-code.fd@file=/path/to/downloaded/edk2-aarch64-code.fd@g' /path/to/qemu-config/podman-machine-default.json`

  where the qemu-config directory is reported by `MachineConfigDir` in the output of `podman machine info`.
- Start machine

Usually these are caused by bad combination of qemu and podman.  We are moving to apple hypervisor for better stabiity.

Sometimes there is a bogus qemu or qcow2 left on the machine and Podman grabs it, or there is a podman installed via brew that conflicts with the podman installed via podman-desktop, hint we want the one installed by podman-desktop.

M1 Pro macOS 14.2.1 `qemu` 8.2.0 `podman` 4.8.2 `podman-desktop` v1.6.3 I followed the following steps using Apple Hypervisor, and got the machine to finally run, but Podman Desktop isn't seeing my initialized machine: ```shell # Uninstall everything brew uninstall podman-desktop podman qemu # Nuke everything else related to podman rm ~/.ssh/podman-machine-default.pub ~/.ssh/podman-machine-default rm -rf ~/.local/share/containers/podman ~/.config/containers/podman # Re-add podman desktop brew install podman-desktop # Install vfkit brew tap cfergeau/crc brew install vfkit # Set machine provider to Apple Hypervisor export CONTAINERS_MACHINE_PROVIDER=applehv # Init & Start podman machine init podman machine start ``` ```shell ~ podman machine ls NAME VM TYPE CREATED LAST UP CPUS MEMORY DISK SIZE podman-machine-default* applehv 3 minutes ago Currently running 5 2GiB 100GiB ``` Is there an intermediary step to follow for the Apple Hypervisor podman machine to show up in Podman Desktop? Edit: There is an intermediary step! Configuring [~/.config/containers/containers.conf](https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/20702) to use `machine.provider="applehv"` allows the `applehv` podman machine to show up in Podman Desktop ```ini [machine] provider="applehv" ```

I solved this by adding following to `~/.config/containers/containers.conf` which @patrick-andrew-jain-taylor mentioned
```toml
[machine]
  provider="applehv"
```
Then I downloaded the `vfkit v0.5.0` binary from https://github.com/crc-org/vfkit/releases/tag/v0.5.0 and placed it in `/usr/local/bin`. After this I could successfully run `podman machine init` & `podman machine start` without having to install podman-desktop.

Switching from qemu to virtual framework solves the issue:
```
brew tap cfergeau/crc
brew install vfkit
export CONTAINERS_MACHINE_PROVIDER=applehv
```
You would need a shell variable to let podman know that you are going to use Apple's hypervisor, this way you can have both vf and qemu (when it works again)

```

I set this up again on an M3 Max, so far it is working well - includes Podman Desktop too working with Apple Hypervisor. Here are the rough steps to clean up and try again (in case you got the "waiting for VM" from multiple installs as I did) Uninstall. ```bash brew uninstall podman podman-desktop ``` Cleanup old keys and machines. ```bash rm -rf ~/.config/containers/ rm -rf ~/.local/share/containers rm ~/.ssh/podman* ``` Reinstall ``` brew install podman podman-desktop ``` Setup podman for apple hypervisor and create machine based on it ```bash export CONTAINERS_MACHINE_PROVIDER=applehv podman machine init podman machine start ``` When this works, you can setup podman-desktop to use applehv (it doesn't use the earlier env var). ```bash # Add the 2 lines below below to ~/.config/containers/containers.conf [machine] provider="applehv" ``` You should be able to run the test hello container and verify podman works and podman-desktop connected to the machine (not using qemu). My next challenge is to make it work with the VSCode Devcontainers extension (and by proxy make it work for neovim), but that is outside the scope of this report.

Removing everything, and installing dmg podman-desktop worked for me.
Here's the exact steps I took.

1. First remove any machines you have

```
podman machine rm --force
```

2. Then remove everything you have installed from homebrew (don't include podman-desktop if you haven't installed it)

```
brew uninstall podman-desktop podman qemu  
```

3. Then remove all podman caches:
```
rm -rf ~/.config/containers  
rm -rf ~/.local/share/containers
rm ~/.ssh/podman*
```

4. Then download podman desktop dmg from: https://podman-desktop.io/downloads

5. Then install podman dmg, It will prompt you that podman is not installed, follow the instruction and install it through podman-desktop. Then it will ask you to create a VM. Follow the instructions to create a VM. Wait for the machine to start.

Now you can go back to developing

Along with the comment here: https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/20776#issuecomment-1852419924 this is what I've done and worked on my side for M3.

_If you are on M1/M2, the recommended path is to update to Podman 4.9._ 

**1 - Remove everything podman and qemu to get a clean environment:**
- remove eventual installation from podman/podman desktop installer from `sudo rm -rf opt/podman` 
- remove brew installations 
  - `brew uninstall podman-desktop`
  - `brew uninstall podman`
  - `brew uninstall qemu`
- cleanup of podman files
  - `rm -rf ~/.ssh/podman-machine-default`     
  - `rm -rf ~/.ssh/podman-machine-default.pub`
  - `rm -rf ~/.local/share/containers`         
  - `rm -rf ~/.config/containers`

**2 - Reinstall using `brew`**
- `brew install podman``

**3 - Install patched EDK2**
- Make sure you have `bunzip2` (if not `brew install bzip2`)
- Make sure QEMU is installed at the following path `/opt/homebrew/Cellar/qemu/8.2.0` (if you are using a different path, just adapt the command below)
- `curl -sL https://github.com/AkihiroSuda/qemu/raw/704f7cad5105246822686f65765ab92045f71a3b/pc-bios/edk2-aarch64-code.fd.bz2 | bunzip2 > /opt/homebrew/Cellar/qemu/8.2.0/share/qemu/edk2-aarch64-code.fd`

Now you should be able to do `podman machine init` and `podman machine start`

You can then reinstall Podman Desktop (either brew or [Podman Desktop website](https://podman-desktop.io/))

The situation should get better with the upcoming release of QEMU 8.2.1

**Note:** If you are on M1/M2, you should be fine with the latest release of Podman 4.9. If not, please raise it!

I don't know what you saw exactly, but while it's true applehv will create a 100GB disk image, it is a sparse image, and it won't use 100GB on disk unless it's filled.
It's doing the equivalent of:
```
$ truncate -s 100G foo.img
$ du -sh foo.img
0
```
